title: Building a CRM (Sales Pipeline) in Laravel
subtitle: Leads, contacts, deals through configurable pipeline stages, an activity timeline, assignment rules, and reporting — the Laravel architecture behind a sales CRM.

sections.h2: How a sales CRM fits together

blocks.p: A CRM is a system of record for relationships and the deals that flow through stages. The design pressure: the pipeline must be configurable per team, every touch must be captured on a unified timeline, and managers need aggregated reporting across thousands of records without N+1 collapse.

blocks.diagram:
Organization
 ├─ Users (sales reps, managers)
 ├─ Contacts ──┐
 ├─ Companies ─┤ (people & accounts)
 ├─ Pipeline
 │   └─ Stages (Lead → Qualified → Proposal → Won/Lost)
 ├─ Deals (value, stage, owner, contact)
 │   └─ Activities (calls, emails, notes, tasks)  ◄ the timeline
 └─ Custom Fields (per-entity, per-org)

blocks.p: The core action — moving a deal through the pipeline — drives stage history, forecasting, and notifications:

blocks.diagram:
Rep drags deal Qualified → Proposal
        │
        ▼  authorize (owns deal / manager)
  Update deal.stage_id + record StageTransition (history)
        │
   ┌────┴─────┐
   ▼          ▼
 Activity   Recompute forecast (queued)
 timeline   + notify manager if value > threshold

ul: Entity model — contacts, companies, deals, with custom fields per organization.
Configurable pipeline — stages are data, not enums, so each team defines its own.
Activity timeline — a polymorphic stream of every interaction on a deal/contact.
Assignment & reporting — routing leads to reps and aggregating pipeline value.

blocks.callout: A CRM is a configurable pipeline over a relationship graph, with a unified activity timeline. The hard parts are user-defined stages/fields (data, not code) and fast aggregate reporting.
blocks.title: The mental model in one sentence

blocks.p: A contact is a person; a company is an account; a contact belongsTo a company. A deal (opportunity) belongsTo a contact/company and an owner, and carries a monetary value and a current stage. These three are the spine of every CRM.

blocks.h3: Pipeline stages as data, not enums

blocks.p: Stages must be configurable per team, so model them as a stages table (name, order, probability%) belonging to a pipeline. A deal references stage_id. Never hard-code stages as a PHP enum — different orgs need different funnels, and admins reorder them at runtime.

Each move appends a stage transition row (deal, from, to, at, by). This history powers velocity metrics (time in stage) and forecasting: expected revenue = Σ(deal value × stage probability). Without history you can only see the current state, not the trend.

Calls, emails, notes, and tasks all appear on one timeline. Model an activities table with a polymorphic subject (subject_type, subject_id) so an activity can attach to a deal, contact, or company. The timeline is a single ordered query, not a union of many tables.

Orgs add their own fields (e.g. "industry", "lead source"). Two patterns: an EAV table (custom_field_values: entity, field_id, value) — flexible but query-heavy; or a JSON column (meta) with a field-definition table for the UI — simpler, queryable via JSON paths in modern MySQL/Postgres. JSON column is usually the right default.
New leads route to reps by rule: round-robin, by territory, or by load. Implement as a queued job that picks the next owner; keep the algorithm in a service so it's testable and swappable.

Dashboards aggregate: pipeline value by stage, deals won per rep, conversion rates. Use database aggregation (selectRaw('stage_id, sum(value)')->groupBy), eager-load relations for lists, and cache expensive rollups. Never loop deals in PHP to sum — push it to SQL.

blocks.callout: Hard-coding pipeline stages as an enum is the classic CRM mistake — it makes the product un-configurable. Stages, fields, and assignment rules must be data the admin controls.
blocks.title: Most common misconception

blocks.p: A CRM tracks contacts, companies, and deals moving through configurable pipeline stages, records every interaction on a polymorphic timeline, and aggregates it all into reports.

A deal is a baton passed down a track of stages. Each lane (pipeline) can have a different number of hurdles because teams sell differently — so the track is built from movable pieces (data), not poured concrete (enums). The timeline is the race commentary: every call, email, and note logged in order. The forecast is the bookmaker's odds: each stage has a probability, and expected revenue weights deal value by how far down the track it is.

head: Approach
Flexibility
Query cost
When
rows: Fields known up front, rarely change

blocks.diagram:
activities
 ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
 │ id  verb    subject_type  subject_id   │
 │ 1   called   Deal          42           │
 │ 2   noted    Contact       7            │
 │ 3   emailed  Deal          42           │
 └───────────────────────────────────────┘
  Deal 42 timeline = where subject=Deal,42 order by created_at

blocks.p: Each lesson is one idea. Code illustrative, not production.

blocks.h3: Lesson 1 — Stages are rows, deals reference them
Lesson 4 — Forecast in SQL, not PHP
Lesson 5 — Round-robin assignment as a service
Lesson 6 — Custom fields via JSON + definitions

items.q: Q1. Why model pipeline stages as a table rather than a PHP enum?
items.a: Stages must be configurable per team — different orgs have different funnels and reorder/add stages at runtime. An enum bakes them into code, making the product un-configurable and every change a deploy.

items.q: Q2. What does a stage-transition history table enable that a single stage_id cannot?
items.a: Trend and velocity analysis: time-in-stage, conversion rates between stages, and historical forecasting. A bare stage_id shows only the current state, not how the deal got there.

items.q: Q3. How does one timeline hold calls, notes, and emails for both deals and contacts?
items.a: A polymorphic activities table (subject_type, subject_id via morphTo). Any entity can have a timeline, and it's a single ordered query rather than a union across separate tables.

items.q: Q4. Give the forecasting formula and where it should run.
items.a: Expected revenue = Σ(deal.value × stage.probability/100) over open deals. Run it in SQL with SUM/JOIN/GROUP BY — never loop deals in PHP to sum.

items.q: Q5. When would you choose a JSON meta column over an EAV table for custom fields?
items.a: Default to JSON meta when fields are admin-defined and filtering is light — it's simpler and queryable via JSON paths. Reach for EAV only when you need heavy per-field reporting/filtering at scale.

items.q: Q6. A deal list page is slow with many rows. First fix?
items.a: Eliminate N+1 by eager-loading contact, owner, and stage (with select of only needed columns) and paginating. Then index foreign keys/status used for filtering.

items.q: Q7. Where does lead-assignment logic belong and why?
items.a: In a dedicated service (e.g. RoundRobinAssigner) called from a queued job — so the algorithm is testable, swappable (round-robin/territory/load), and off the request hot path.

blocks.p: Re-do the quiz from memory and re-derive the timeline + forecast at each session.

head: When
Session (~10–15 min)
Goal
rows: Read packet; quiz with answers open.
Quiz closed-book; redraw the polymorphic timeline.
Flashcards; explain stages-as-data vs enum aloud.
Interleaved quiz; write the forecast query from blank.
Teach the pipeline/forecast model to someone.
Closed-book interleaved pass; misses restart Day 1.

blocks.callout: Retrieving just before you'd forget produces the strongest gain.
blocks.title: Why the gaps grow

blocks.p: Topics shuffled to train choosing the right idea.

items.q: M1. A manager wants 'average days a deal spends in Proposal'. Which table answers it and how?
items.a: The stage-transition history: for each deal, diff timestamps of entering and leaving the Proposal stage, then average. Impossible with only the current stage_id.

items.q: M2. Reporting dashboard times out on a large org. Two architectural fixes?
items.a: (1) Aggregate in SQL with GROUP BY and proper indexes (stage_id, owner_id, status, created_at). (2) Cache/precompute rollups (scheduled job or materialized summary table) instead of recomputing per request.

items.q: M3. Admins ask to add a 'Renewal' stage between Won and Closed mid-quarter. What breaks if stages were an enum?
items.a: A code change + migration + deploy, and existing forecasts/history keyed to enum values get messy. With stages-as-data it's an insert and a reorder — no deploy.

items.q: M4. Sales wants to filter deals by a custom 'industry' field. You used a JSON meta column — how, and the caveat?
items.a: whereJsonContains('meta->industry', ...) (MySQL/Postgres JSON path). Caveat: JSON-path filters are slower and may not use ordinary indexes — add a generated/virtual column + index, or move hot filters to real columns.

items.q: M5. Two reps both 'claim' the same inbound lead simultaneously. How prevent double assignment?
items.a: Make assignment atomic: a queued job with a DB transaction + row lock (or a unique constraint on lead+owner / a status flag updated conditionally) so only the first claim wins.

items.q: M6. When is EAV worth its join cost over a JSON column?
items.a: When you need heavy, indexed per-field querying/reporting across many admin-defined fields at scale — EAV lets you index field values relationally, whereas JSON paths get expensive. For most CRMs, JSON wins on simplicity.

ul: Build the pipeline twice — stages as enum vs stages as data — and feel why data wins the moment an admin reorders.
Break reporting on purpose — sum deals in PHP across 50k rows, watch it crawl, then move it to SQL aggregation.
Write velocity tests first on stage history before building the dashboard.
Add email integration — log inbound/outbound email as timeline activities (mailbox parsing or webhook).
Stretch goals: deal forecasting by close date, lead deduplication/merge, territory-based assignment, custom-field validation rules, and a saved-views/filters system.

flashcards: The two hard parts of a CRM
User-defined pipeline stages/fields (data not code) and fast aggregate reporting over many records.
Why stages must be a table
Teams have different funnels and reorder stages at runtime; an enum forces a deploy for every change.
What stage-transition history enables
Velocity (time-in-stage), conversion rates, and historical forecasting — invisible to a bare stage_id.
Forecast formula
Σ(deal.value × stage.probability/100) over open deals — computed in SQL.
How one timeline serves many entities
Polymorphic activities table (subject_type/subject_id via morphTo).
Default custom-field strategy
JSON meta column + a field-definition table for UI/validation; EAV only for heavy per-field reporting.
Filter a JSON custom field
whereJsonContains('meta->key', value) — add a generated column + index for hot filters.
Reporting rule of thumb
Aggregate in SQL (SUM/GROUP BY) with indexes; cache expensive rollups; never sum in PHP loops.
Lead assignment placement
A service (round-robin/territory/load) invoked from a queued job — testable and swappable.
Preventing double lead assignment
Atomic claim: transaction + row lock or conditional status update so only the first wins.
List deals without N+1
Eager-load contact/owner/stage with selected columns, then paginate.
Contact vs company vs deal
Contact = person, company = account, deal = opportunity with value + stage; deal belongs to contact/company + owner.
glossary: Contact
A person in the CRM, usually belonging to a company.
Company / Account
An organization that contacts and deals attach to.
Deal / Opportunity
A potential sale with a value, owner, and current stage.
Pipeline
An ordered set of stages a deal moves through.
Stage
A step in the pipeline with an order and win-probability.
Stage transition
A history row recording a deal moving from one stage to another.
Forecast
Expected revenue = value weighted by stage probability.
Activity
A logged interaction (call/email/note/task) on the timeline.
Polymorphic relation
One relation (morphTo) letting activities attach to any entity.
Custom field
An admin-defined attribute stored in JSON meta or EAV.
EAV
Entity-Attribute-Value table pattern for flexible fields.
Round-robin
Assignment rotating leads evenly across reps.
